sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Back pain is common and is often due to benign musculoskeletal causes, but clinicians must recognise spinal emergencies and important non-spinal mimics that may progress to permanent neurological injury or life-threatening disease.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Recent onset pain without major trauma
- Severe or progressive pain
- Bilateral sciatic pain
- Urinary retention or incontinence
- Bowel incontinence
- Lower-extremity neurological deficit
- Fever, chills, night sweats, or weight loss
- Saddle sensory change

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- New, chronic, or previously diagnosed back pain can still represent a spinal emergency
- Red flags include cancer history, immunosuppression, infection risk, IV drug use, steroid use, and cauda equina features
- Consider visceral mimics such as pancreatitis, aortic aneurysm, renal colic, or cardiac causes

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Conduct secondary survey
- Consider simple analgesia if appropriate
- Consider inhaled analgesia if necessary
- Position for comfort and avoid forced movements
- Ensure appropriate follow-up where available

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access if indicated
- Consider fluids if clinically indicated
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Consider further analgesia in line with local protocol and scope
- Consider ECG where indicated

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Assessment of back pain is complex and requires correlation of pain features, neurological findings, and red flags
- Full history, including change from baseline in chronic conditions, is important
